headlines.title: Fatal crash blocks westbound Bay Bridge toll lanes
headlines.description: A fatal crash was reported Thursday morning on westbound Interstate Highway 80 near the Bay Bridge, according to the California Highway Patrol. The crash happened on the freeway’s section west of the Metering Lights. A lane was blocked due to a crash, the CHP said on social media around 6:15 a.m. Patrol officers did not provide an estimate of reopening immediately. Motorists are advised to expect delays in the area. This is a developing story. Please check back for updates.

headlines.title: Sold-out BottleRock to bring 120,000 to Napa for weekend of food, pop icons, local DJs
headlines.description: It’s the party of the year: 80-plus bands, celebrity chefs, a silent disco, comedians, wine from Napa’s biggest names, even a spa. From Friday to Sunday, 120,000-plus music lovers and partiers are expected to turn up at this year’s BottleRock Napa Valley festival and afterparties around Napa. The festival is a variety pack of pop culture icons and artists on seven stages around the 36-acre Napa Valley Expo near the city’s downtown. Rapper Lil Wayne’s set is right before ’80s Australian rockers Men at Work. TikTok chef QCP and Jersey Shore’s DJ Pauly D share the Williams Sonoma Culinary Stage. DJ sets and vendors are where the Bay Area talent shines. Lumpia from Cooked by Gio and sushi by Eiko’s Napa will feed the hungry masses. Veteran Bay Area radio DJ Kevvy Kev holds down a silent disco and DJ Umami plays hip hop and R&B between sets on the culinary stage. DJ Tylawave, aka North Bay native Tyla Rae Jones, will spin records Sunday afternoon over margaritas at the Patron Vinyl Hacienda tent. She plans to bring a mix from her collection, playing “Funk, soul and contemporary jams,” she said, adding, “I always play Grace Jones.” In 2010, noting a male-dominated DJ scene in San Francisco, Jones founded Where My Ladies At, an all-femme DJ collective. BottleRock will be her biggest festival yet. Before tickets sold out in April, a single-day general admission cost $252, with a three-day VIP pass priced over $1,300. Napa County Jan 13 Napa Feb 24 A report coordinated by the city of Napa and Latitude 38 shows that revenue from the 2025 BottleRock and La Onda festivals combined added up to $2.1 million in revenue for city services. The festival was started in 2013 by Latitude 38, and Live Nation Entertainment acquired a majority stake in BottleRock in 2017. In February, Latitude 38 announced the last-minute cancellation of the third-ever La Onda, BottleRock’s Latin music counterpart. In 2025, La Onda drew nearly 60,000 attendees. Many in Napa stand to gain from the bustling BottleRock weekend. According to a city contract with Latitude 38, the hourly wage for a Napa police officer increases for the weekend, coming out to $215. In total, Latitude 38’s bill for fire and police is estimated at $403,468.13.

headlines.title: San Rafael teacher accused of illegal relationship with student
headlines.description: A San Rafael teacher was arrested Wednesday on allegations of an illegal and inappropriate relationship with a minor, police said. Investigators identified the teacher as Kevin Eric Allen, 35. Allen has been suspended from his job at San Rafael High School since the allegations were reported, according to the San Rafael Police Department. Police said school administrators told investigators on April 14 after learning of a possible illegal and inappropriate relationship between Allen and a person under age 18. School officials suspended Allen and began a parallel internal investigation while cooperating with police. They interviewed the victim and witnesses and executed search warrants to review communications between Allen and the minor. Based on evidence gathered during the investigation, police determined an illegal and inappropriate relationship had allegedly occurred. Allen was arrested at his San Rafael residence and booked into Marin County Jail on suspicion of arranging to meet a minor for sexual intent, annoying or molesting a minor under 18, and violating the terms of his Marin County probation, police said. Police asked anyone with information about this case to contact the San Rafael Police Department at (415) 485-3000. Tips can also be made online at srpd.org/tips.

headlines.title: Crews contain brush fire near neighborhood in Contra Costa County
headlines.description: Fire crews contained a small brush fire near a Contra Costa County neighborhood Wednesday evening, according to the Contra Costa County Fire Protection District. Fire officials said the fire occurred in a neighborhood near Concord at around 7:45 p.m. Authorities said some homes were threatened at some point, but crews were setting up to protect them, especially those near Butternut Way. Firefighters said Officials said the fire grew up to 2 acres, and it had a slow rate of speed before it was contained. Fire officials added no homes were burned.

headlines.title: 2026 Memorial Day weekend travel expected to break records
headlines.description: A record number of Americans are expected to travel over the Memorial Day weekend despite higher fuel prices, according to AAA. AAA estimates nearly 6 million people will travel to and within California between Thursday and Monday, making it one of the busiest Memorial Day travel periods on record. San Francisco ranked as the sixth most popular destination in the United States, AAA said. Most travelers are expected to drive at least 50 miles from home, even though gas prices are more than $1 higher on average than they were at this time last year. NBC Bay Area’s Kris Sanchez has the full report in the video above.

headlines.title: Raul Castro's indictment brings mixed feelings to Cuban American community in SF
headlines.description: The Cuban American community is spread out over the Bay Area, but some in San Francisco said the collective reaction to what’s happening is not easy to analyze. The Mission District in San Francisco is preparing for the Carnaval festivities and the Carnaval parade this weekend, but what’s happening in Cuba is casting a cloud over the joy. The area in the Mission around 24th and Bryant is where the Carnaval Parade will kick off Sunday morning. It is part of one of the Bay Area’s longest running, colorful, multicultural street festivals and the Cuban American community joins enthusiastically. But the general manager of the popular Cubita restaurant on Mission Street, Daniel Said, says the Trump administration’s federal indictment against Raul Castro has impacted the mood, stirring up mixed feelings in a community with many Cuban exiles. “I think there has been such a large disconnect between the Cuban American community and Cubans as a whole, just from what’s going on in our past country to country, that it just seems like another barrier to overcome,” Said said. Cubita workers also point out Cuba’s people are struggling with sanctions by the Trump administration as well as problems caused by the Iran war. Recently, several DJ’s from Cubita held a fundraiser and are currently in Cuba to help families there. “Gave them the necessities, basic things like being able to get water, food and things like that, for us it does hit us personally we do know people that are struggling there right now despite the grand scheme of what’s happening ‘politically’ but it ends up being very on the ground level that we need to really help and support the people that we know,” said Vincent Chooi, a Cubita worker. The Cubita general manager also says while the community has mixed feelings about Castro, in his opinion, they’re skeptical of President Trump’s motives as well. “Yeah, I think it’s all revolved around them, right? It’s all very self-serving and in the long run, is it helping the Cuban people as a whole? Probably not,” Said said. Participants say they still expect the carnival celebrations to be as joyous as they always are but they say they will be keeping an eye on what’s happening in Cuba.

headlines.title: Wildfire risk drives FAIR Plan insurance hikes in California
headlines.description: Homeowners in wildfire-prone areas of California insured through the FAIR Plan could soon see higher insurance premiums. The FAIR Plan, which stands for Fair Access to Insurance Requirements, was created by the state as an insurer of last resort for homeowners unable to get coverage on the private market, often because they live in areas at high risk for wildfires. Starting in October, FAIR Plan rates are expected to rise by some of the largest amounts in years, with some of the biggest increases expected in Orinda. The San Francisco Chronicle reports premiums in Orinda are expected to increase by an average of 31%, which could mean homeowners paying hundreds or even thousands of dollars more each year. A spokesperson for the California FAIR Plan Association said not every customer will see increases that large, but policyholders whose homes face significant wildfire risk are expected to see the steepest hikes. NBC Bay Area’s Bob Redell has more in the video above. Contra Costa County May 17 PG&E May 16 wildfires May 7 California pushes wildfire preparedness with blazes on the rise

headlines.title: Popular spot in San Jose loses thousands of dollars after break-in
headlines.description: A popular spot in San Jose is out thousands of dollars after thieves broke in, stole cash and left significant damage behind. Video shared by the business owner shows two people smashing their way into Burbanks Bites and Billiards, a bar and grill on Bascom Avenue. Daniel Ramirez, co-owner of the business, says it happened Sunday morning at around 6:30 am. Owners told NBC Bay Area this is the sixth time this has happened to them in the two years they’ve been open; however, in the past, Ramirez says thieves have stolen bottles of liquor – this time, they got into the safe. Between the damage and the money stolen, they’re looking at about $10,000 in losses. “It honestly broke my heart, I teared up a little bit, the cash I have in there is to pay people, vendors, I had to do it out of pocket at some point,” Ramirez said. A couple of businesses nearby told NBC Bay Area they’ve also been broken into in the past. The taco spot next door said it’s happened to them a few times over the last couple of years. “It’s starting to become a normal thing,” Ramirez said. After hearing what happened, the community is now showing support to the business. “It’s one of my favorite spots to go to and the food is absolutely amazing,” Daniel, a frequent customer, said. “They do so much to give this type of environment to us, and for them to do this, it’s just no respect, it’s sad that’s how society is.” Now Ramirez is taking more precautions. He’s installed extra cameras. “I think we have to do the gates inside, people see windows and they see gates inside and they get discouraged. But it’s sad that we have to come down to that,” he said. Ramirez said he did file a police report. NBC Bay Area reached out to San Jose police but hasn’t heard back. In a statement Michael Mulcahy, who represents this area, said his office will be working to make sure this incident and the area receives the attention they deserve.

headlines.title: California fishermen turn to new ‘pop-up' gear to save whales — and their jobs
headlines.description: Thousands of fishing traps lost at sea are damaging ocean ecosystems and threatening marine life, including whales, sharks, turtles, and seabirds, according to wildlife experts. “It’s a really massive problem that requires interesting and scalable solutions,” said Neil Nathan, a marine scientist and researcher at The Benioff Ocean Science Laboratory at UC Santa Barbara. “It does pose a huge risk and threat to a wide variety of marine life here in California.” It’s a really massive problem that requires interesting and scalable solutions. Neil Nathan, a marine scientist Conventional fishing traps – aimed at catching lobster, crabs, and shrimp – are set on the seafloor and are attached to rope and buoys so fishermen can locate and retrieve them later. Those dangling ropes can sometimes stretch hundreds of feet long and because the traps can be left out at sea for days at a time, vulnerable wildlife can get ensnared, including threatened and even endangered whales. Roughly 500 whales entangled by fishing gear Fishing gear has entangled close to 500 whales off the west coast over the past two decades, leaving some dead, according to records from NOAA obtained and analyzed by the NBC Bay Area Investigative Unit. “All it takes is one spin and [the gear] becomes attached to the whale,” said Brand Little, a California fisherman of more than 20 years. “We can’t seem to keep our gear off the whales.” Little says while entanglements have been detrimental to wildlife, they’ve also been devastating for fishermen, who’ve watched their crabbing seasons get cut shorter and shorter each year in an effort to protect those majestic mammals. “We’ve given up months in the beginning of the season, while the whales are still here before they migrate south,” Little said. “We’ve given up months on the end of the season to get the gear out of the water before the whales come back.” Brand Little is a California fisherman of more than 20 years. Shorter crab seasons squeeze fishermen A season that used to last roughly eight months has, in recent years, been reduced to sometimes less than three. But now, for the first time, California is giving fishermen the chance to keep fishing after the close of the regular crab season if they use a new type of equipment known as pop-up gear. Unlike standard traps, pop-up gear allows the cages to be set without leaving dangling rope behind. Fishermen use GPS coordinates to find the traps once it’s time to retrieve them and then send out an acoustic signal that triggers the traps to release a buoy and rope back to the surface so the gear can be reeled in. The rope shoots up in seconds But the new technology for the old cages costs about $1,200 per device. Several traps can be grouped together to share one of the new pop-ups, but depending on how many traps you have, some fishermen could still need upwards of 20 of those new devices. “It’s a big investment. It’s $20,000, but that buys us the ability to go fishing,” said Little, who was one of the first fishermen to begin experimenting with the gear in 2023 and now uses the gear to catch crab and shrimp. “Eventually, the writing’s on the wall.” It’s a big investment. It’s $20,000, but that buys us the ability to go fishing. Brand Little, describing his decision to use pop-up gear to catch crab and shrimp Brand Little prepares to use summon his fishing traps by sending out an acoustic signal that triggers the traps to release a rope and buoy to the surface. Just this month, the California Dept. of Fish and Wildlife officially authorized the use of pop-up gear for fishermen targeting Dungeness crab off the coast of the San Francisco Bay Area. The equipment received approval for use in more southern parts of California, along Monterey and Morro Bay, in April. Of California’s roughly 350 active crab fishermen, about one in five is already using the new pop-up gear, according to the California Dept. of Fish and Wildlife. The state also recently approved the new technology for shrimping, but regulators haven’t gotten any bites from crews wanting to use the gear to catch lobster. However, with this being the equipment’s inaugural season for crab and shrimp, it may not be long before pop-up gear manages to reel in even more fishermen. Pop-up gear offers fisherman a high-tech alternative to traditional crab traps, which have long been tied to length ropes left dangling out at sea for days at a time. Pop-up gear, however, allows traps to sit on the sea floor without that dangling rope. Once it’s time to retrieve the traps, fishermen use GPS to locate the cages and then send out an acoustic signal to trigger the pop-up gear to release a buoy tied to rope that floats to the surface so fishermen can reel in the trap. Lost at sea The aim of the gear is to dramatically reduce the amount of time those ropes attached to traps are left dangling out at sea, where they could unintentionally entangle wildlife or get severed by oncoming boats or storms. “The weather is super rough…and when a boat drives across, the buoy gets cut off,” Little said. “And you’re like, where is it? It’s gone.” Historically, California fishermen lose more than 6,000 crab and lobster traps each year, according to government records the Investigative Unit obtained from the California Dept. of Fish and Wildlife. These so-called “ghost traps” can remain lost at sea for years, inadvertently capturing and killing all sorts of marine life, including sharks and seabirds. “Essentially every trap that is down there has the potential to be killing marine life,” Nathan said. “All these animals can get stuck and trapped in these traps and die.” Senior Investigative Reporter Bigad Shaban joined marine scientists and researchers on a boating expedition to recover lost fishing traps off the coast of Santa Barbara. All aboard Nathan and his team invited Senior Investigative Reporter Bigad Shaban to join them on one of their expeditions to recover lost traps about 20 miles off the coast of Santa Barbara. Going off tips from local divers, researchers believed ghost traps could be down below. But along the seafloor, visibility was often murky. “It’s almost like being on a scavenger hunt down there,” Nathan said. “It’s dark, there’s algae, there’s kelp, there are rocks covering things. It can be a bit challenging to find them.” Neil Nathan, a marine scientist and researcher with UC Santa Barbara’s Marine Science Institute, is working to find and recover fishing traps lost at sea. Training artificial intelligence how to fish for ghost traps Which is why scientists are now working to train artificial intelligence to analyze sonar technology to spot the ghost traps autonomously and then provide the exact coordinates to researchers. “It’ll make us a lot more efficient in finding these traps when underwater,” Nathan said. “These traps have a very unique signature on sonar, given that they are rectangularly shaped and made of metal.” When scientists started reeling in the abandoned fishing gear, they found many filled with marine life, including lobster, which the traps are actually designed to catch. Yet, with no one coming to collect them, they would just die in the traps and become bait for the next lobster. Impacts to one specific species can trickle down and have impacts on the rest of the ecosystem. Neil Nathan, speaking about the potential harm caused by abandoned fishing gear known as ‘ghost traps’ The cycle could, unfortunately, repeat itself for years, at the detriment of fishermen and the environment. “Impacts to one specific species can trickle down and have impacts on the rest of the ecosystem,” Nathan said. “[Ghost traps] are presenting a clear and ever-present risk to the marine life.” Scientists retrieved a dozen ghost traps during the trip off the coast, but thousands more will likely be added to the ocean floor by the end of the year. Thus, however many traps researchers are able to recover, they will likely only represent a small fraction of what remains out at sea. Researchers and scientists at UC Santa Barbara recovered more than 30 ghost lobster traps over the course of a five-day search effort out at sea. The escape hatch California law requires the metal cages be outfitted with trap doors that eventually disintegrate to give sea life an exit if the trap is lost. “Each trap destruct device shall create an unobstructed escape opening in the top or upper half of the trap of at least five inches in diameter,” state California regulations. Nathan and his team, however, have found traps still in one piece months or even years after they were originally set. “This trap is still sealed shut,” Nathan points out while providing a tour of the recently recovered ghost traps. “The hinges — they’re keeping this door attached — they should be degrading faster.” California law requires fishing traps to partially disintegrate when lost at sea in order to prevent marine sea from being unintentionally caught and killed. Marine scientists, however, argue those materials don’t always break down, leaving some traps fully intact, without an escape hatch, months or even years after they were set. California law requires the metal cages be outfitted with trap doors that eventually disintegrate to give sea life an exit if the trap is lost. In hopes of creating fishing cages that better comply with state regulations, researchers at UC Santa Barbara have been working on a new kind of biodegradable material for fishing gear. “We’ve been trying to think of ways to replace those petroleum-based plastics with other materials,” said Alyson Santoro, a microbial oceanographer who has been leading the research. “By some estimates, there may be more plastic than fish in the ocean by the year 2050, so finding different types of materials to use in the ocean is really, really important.” Alyson Santoro (left) is an oceanographer and UC Santa Barbara professor who specializes in microorganisms. Her research is focusing on how to construct certain types of fishing equipment out of biodegradable materials, in part, to reduce the amount of plastic pollution in the ocean. Preventing ghost traps In addition to potentially reducing the amount of plastic pollution in the ocean, the research could ultimately lead to creating a new type of trap door that more reliably disintegrates when the trap is lost at sea. “When you are having potentially thousands and thousands of traps lost every year and accumulating in these ecosystems, it’s too large of a challenge to solve with cleanup alone,” Nathan said. “We really need to invest more time and energy in solutions to how we prevent trap loss in the first place.” Some of the largest and most vulnerable species just may depend on it. At stake is a huge environmental cost and an economic one, according to Brand Little. The entire commercial fishing industry is at stake. Brand Little, California fisherman “It’s a lot of opportunity lost,” Little said. “The entire commercial fishing industry is at stake.” Little says it’s not just about the price of replacing missing gear. He says he’s also lost out on the ability to catch more seafood. When a trap goes missing, fishermen aren’t allowed to set additional traps until the next season. “It’s literally cut our crab business in half,” he said. Little says the losses can add up to tens of thousands of dollars. But ever since he started using the pop-up gear, he tells us he hasn’t lost a single trap. “I don’t want to have to pay to use it, but I’m going to sink without it –so you adapt or die,” Little said. “It gives us a lifeline and it may be the only thing that saves the industry.” The lead imagery at the top of this story were provided courtesy of Pieter Folkens (NOAA Permit #18786). Contact The Investigative Unit submit tips | 1-888-996-TIPS | e-mail Bigad Follow @BigadShaban on social media Bigad Shaban
headlines.source: Bigad Shaban, Michael Horn and Alex Bozovic

headlines.title: Zuckerberg warns ‘success isn't a given' after laying off 10% of Meta
headlines.description: As Meta cut 10% of its workforce on Wednesday — a move that had been anticipated for more than a month — CEO Mark Zuckerberg addressed the tech giant’s transition in a companywide memo. Zuckerberg thanked the impacted employees, stressed the importance of artificial intelligence, assured workers there shouldn’t be additional cuts in 2026 and laid out some of his vision for the future. Along with the cuts, Meta is redirecting 7,000 employees into AI roles, NBC News reported Tuesday. “But success isn’t a given,” Zuckerberg warned. “AI is the most consequential technology of our lifetimes. The companies that lead the way will define the next generation,” Zuckerberg said in the note. The memo was posted on X by a New York Times reporter and a source familiar with the matter confirmed its authenticity to NBC News. A leak last month about upcoming layoffs prompted Meta executives to share, shortly before reporting first-quarter 2026 earnings, that the tech giant was gearing up to lay off around 10% of the company’s employees. The reorganization, which Meta first detailed in an internal memo in April, includes cutting about 8,000 employees and not filling approximately 6,000 open positions. Meta previously confirmed the April announcement’s authenticity to NBC News. The employees affected by this restructuring were informed on Wednesday. In April, Meta explained some of the calculus behind its shifting financials, noting that it was increasing 2026 capital expenditures to between $125 billion and $145 billion. That was because of “expectations for higher component pricing this year and, to a lesser extent, additional data center costs to support future year capacity,” according to Meta’s first-quarter 2026 report. “This is the most dynamic I have seen our industry. I’m optimistic about everything we’re building to give billions of people the power to express themselves and connect with the people they care about,” Zuckerberg said in his Wednesday note. “We’re transforming our company to make sure it will always be the best place for talented people to have the greatest impact.” Zuckerberg said he’s “grateful to those leaving today” and added that the company hasn’t been as transparent as he would have liked. He said that’s something he hopes Meta works on. Employees in the United States who were laid off will receive severance including four months’ pay, with additional weeks for each year they were employed by Meta, according to the April memo sent by the head of people, Janelle Gale. Additional support, including for immigration and healthcare, is expected to be made available as well. social media May 4 social media Mar 25 Meta and YouTube found liable of negligence in landmark social media addiction trial

headlines.title: Advocates for curbing public drug use say San Francisco is making progress
headlines.description: Wednesday evening, San Francisco leaders and community members gathered in the South of Market neighborhood to celebrate what they say is a step in the right direction in curbing overdoses and addiction. They’re citing the city’s recent shift from tolerance of public drug use to pushing people towards recovery and accountability. At Montesacro Italian restaurant on Stevenson Street, the advocacy group Druf Free Streets held a celebration to mark its first year of action. The restaurant has been in the SOMA neighborhood for 11 years. Owner Gianluca Legrottaglie, said shortly after the restaurant came to the area, “fentanyl came right after, and it became this devastating phenomenon, and now we are dealing with devastating consequences for letting it spread so widely.” Legrottaglie said that over that time, he watched public drug use become more widespread and impact local businesses. He noted that many of the businesses that used to be on the street with him are no longer there. However, he has noticed a recent change. “Over the past year, there’s definitely been a dramatic difference with the conditions of the streets, especially these alleys around 6th street, especially with Jessie Street,” he said. State Assemblymember Matt Haney, who represents San Francisco and lives in the Tenderloin, said he has also noticed a difference. “It used to be in many of these blocks around here, there were people who were using drugs, selling drugs everywhere you looked, needles on the ground, and you’re not seeing nearly as much of that anymore,” he continued. Haney, who used to represent San Francisco’s Tenderloin as a supervisor, explained that San Francisco has gone through a significant shift in approach during recent years. “No more do you see San Francisco with an approach of, ‘Here, we’re going to give you a needle, we’re going to help you use in a safer way,’ no, it should be, ‘We’re gonna help you get off those drugs,'” Haney said. Haney added that he is trying once again to pass a bill that would free up state funding to support drug-free housing. At the city level, Supervisor Matt Dorsey is pushing forward legislation that would prioritize city funds for permanent supportive housing that bans onsite drug use. Dorsey, who is open about his own recovery from addiction, was present at the celebration on Wednesday, as was San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie. Lurie praised the work of Drug Free Streets and of other community members who helped him learn more about recovery. Lurie shared with the audience, “I have to tell you that I am a little emotional, because I spent a couple of hours last night doing a ride-along with our SFPD, and I went to Mission and 16th, and I went to 6th street, and I went through the Tenderloin, and,” Lurie paused, “we have a lot of work to do.” “There are still way too many people openly using, suffering; they are in pain,” he continued. Also speaking at the celebration on Wednesday was Amber Richmond, who described the years she spent openly using drugs in the streets. “Every day revolved around getting high,” she explained. Richmond said she currently works helping people to get housing through the very same system she had to go through. “I can tell you, there are still major gaps for people who are trying to direct their own recovery, people who want to move forward, rebuild their lives, and create stability, beyond just surviving day to day,” she said. Richmond emphasized that it took more than getting housing to help her break the cycle of addiction. ,”I want to be clear, though, compassion matters, housing matters, treatment matters, and people deserve dignity, support, and care, but recovery matters just as much,” she said. “Housing alone was not enough for me, I’d been housed for 6 years, but only recently had I truly begun rebuilding my life through recovery,” she continued. Legrottaglie said he wants to see the city and local advocates keep up the momentum on this issue. “We don’t want to go anywhere, but we are asking for more support,” he said.

headlines.title: New housing proposal in San Francisco's Outer Richmond sparks debate
headlines.description: Safeway and a development partner have supplied new plans to replace an existing market in favor of a seven-story housing with a new grocery store in San Francisco’s Outer Richmond neighborhood. This proposal could usher in a new era on a section of La Playa Street in the Outer Richmond, where Safeway is currently located. The store would stay, but housing would be built above. “I think it looks great very modern, very stylish architecture wise it’s also good,” said Yefim, a resident. This month, Safeway’s parent company and align real estate put in an application to develop an 85-foot development with housing, a garage and a brand-new grocery store. But with the store torn down during construction, some are worried about where they’ll shop. And the possibility of more cars in the neighborhood with the upgraded store and added housing. “Me personally because, I have been here many years, I don’t like it because of the traffic,” said Laura, a resident. “I get people are used to how they get around, they’re used to the way the neighborhood is, at the same time we have a housing shortage. We haven’t built nearly enough homes on that side, at least,” said Jane Natoli, a volunteer organizer with Grow the Richmond. Natoli says it’s important to mitigate impacts, but also points out the proposal meets a need. “It’s great to see actually since they initially discussed it they actually added more homes and more affordable homes as well they have upped that percentage, so its good to see another step forward, excited to see some more new homes coming to a neighborhood that has not traditionally built that many,” Natoli said. The plan includes more than 500 units. In a statement, a representative with align real estate said in part: “With great transit access and views of Ocean Beach, these new homes will make it possible for more working families, seniors and young people to live and work in this amazing community.” It’s not clear yet when the city might approve the project, but grocery store customers told NBC Bay Area they’re watching what happens because this location works for them.
